why_historic: One of Seattle's eight officially designated historic districts; well-preserved early-1900s streetcar commercial core and surrounding residential housing stock.
Olmsted-planned early-20th-century streetcar neighborhood with boulevards and period homes; directly adjacent to the historic 1915 golf course.
Contains the Olmsted Brothers-designed Jefferson Park course (opened 1915), the first municipally owned golf course in Seattle; older Beacon Hill housing.
Ballard Avenue Historic District is on the National Register and is a designated Seattle landmark district; 19th/early-20th-century maritime townscape.
Historic Admiral district and Alki Point, site of the 1851 Denny Party landing; older West Seattle homes.
Adjacent ZIP containing the municipal course (opened 1940); established pre-war and mid-century neighborhoods.
Older north-Seattle housing stock near the historic Sand Point area; Jackson Park is a long-established municipal course (1930).
Historic downtown Snoqualmie and Snoqualmie Falls; National Register sites in the valley. TPC Snoqualmie Ridge also nearby.
Historic early-1900s small-town downtown founded on logging and dairy; intact main-street character with its own municipal course.
National Register-listed former coal-mining town with preserved historic district and museum; nearest course is in adjacent Maple Valley.
Historic downtown Bothell with preserved Main Street buildings and Wayne area; nearest full course is Inglewood in adjacent Kenmore.
Less a historic district than established mid-century Eastside character; Bellevue Municipal is a long-standing classic course (1930s origins).
